item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't see how that should reclassify the SCP. Danger is a minor part of Object Class- what's more important is how hard something is to contain. The reason that higher object classes tend to be more dangerous is because "more dangerous" is often an easy way to make something harder to contain; it's not easy to execute a SCP that is both mostly harmless yet escapes containment at the drop of a hat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hi i suggest adding an interesting thing that may reclassify this scp. If you shove inside a large amount of radioactive material like plutonium, the acellerated rate of decay will release all the energy contained inside, basically killing everything around it that was not shielded and contaminating the area to any levels. Depending on material and time settings it may be well possible to use it as doomsday device, place it on empire state, with uranium block, make it run at max power for several hours and there would be nothing in the city alive.

item.title: Re: Scientific inquiry #2
item.content:encoded: I appreciate the input. And yes, that does make sense in the fact that it would probably kill humans as well as 682

item.title: Re: Scientific inquiry #2
item.content:encoded:
+ Here's that test
- Here's that test

Termination Test Record: SCP-682 tissue sample was affixed to end of a steel rod, and the rod was used by D-682-40 to open SCP-241. SCP-241 showed different recipes than the previous time it was opened. Of the 99 recipes, three were in unknown languages, two were incomprehensible "word salad", and one was a memetic kill agent. Of the remaining 93 recipes, 100% had at least one instruction or ingredient which made it impossible to follow the recipe with complete faithfulness. Examples include:

  • "Chill to -10,000F° before serving."
  • "Garnish with antimatter parsley."
  • "Marinate for 900 trillion years."

D-682-41 was tasked with cooking the 93 usable recipes, using SCP-241 plus a "correction sheet" (written by Dr. █████) which replaced impossible ingredients and instructions with the closest feasible analogue, with the results being delivered to SCP-682 containment cell upon completion. SCP-682 has shown no ill effects after having eaten all 93 meals.

My question is, what is stopping the Foundation from combining that recipe in SCP-241 with SCP-119?

Maybe it's because of that test with 2 million years? The one that created an ecosystem from just water and air, and killed a researcher with some type of unknown spores.

And the recipe states that something has to be marinated for 900 trillion years. I think it surely will kill 682, but humans will also die.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: reminds me of the 4chan post where someone called a minute (60 seconds) "microwave time" and everyone made fun of him for it.

item.title: Scientific inquiry #2
item.content:encoded: I may be remembering incorrectly (I also hope that I am not in the wrong discussion area) but, and again, only if I am remebering correctly, there is a termination attempt for SCP-682 involving SCP-241. What they found inside were various "impossible" and "improbable" recipes for killing that damnable lizard. One of them, I believ, involved letting something stew for a ludicrous amount of time (something like 3 trillion years? [citation needed]). My question is, what is stopping the Foundation from combining that recipe in SCP-241 with SCP-119?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, sorry. I may have misread that. Thought "the adjusted" time would mean the amount of minutes put on the timecrowave itself

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, it got hungry and ate.

the accelerated time experienced within SCP-119 is not accelerated from the perspective of those being affected, instead causing the occupants to perceive that they are simply staying inside of the microwave for the adjusted duration

So, if you input 60 seconds and choose power level 4, those 60 seconds will become 5 months, which is the adjusted time.

For the rat, it felt like 5 months have passed while it was inside the microwave, but for researchers it was 60 seconds.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Addendum: After subsequent testing, it has been determined that the accelerated time experienced within SCP-119 is not accelerated from the perspective of those being affected, instead causing the occupants to perceive that they are simply staying inside of the microwave for the adjusted duration

CONTENTS: Rattus Norvegicus (common lab rat), small cage with lining, external automatic food/water dispenser filled with 5 months of food/water attached to tubes routed through air vent.TIME INPUT: 60POWER LEVEL: 4 TEST RESULTS: Upon starting the timer, subject became a blur, rapidly moving throughout its cage. Both the food and water supplies drained from their containers rapidly.

This does not add up for me, how could the rats even manage to eat?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely an interesting article, though I think it should be categorized as Safe since you can lock it in a box and be done with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can see this thing being used by the Manna Charitable Foundation and by MC&D very easily.
MCF could use it to age dangerous radioactive materials until the materials are no longer radioactive.
MC&D could brew very expensive alcoholic beverages in a matter of seconds.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Since the article is not listed as a collaboration, you will need to get the author's permission (in this case, elrosth) in order to modify the article or testing log.

However, you are welcome to write a tale describing your experiment as a separate article. It won't be linked to on the SCP-119 page without author permission again, but an interesting enough result/story will survive on its own.

item.title: Testing Request
item.content:encoded: Even though I am a bit late, may i wright some test logs to this? (I really would like to try testing with SCP-119 having had the connections to the magnetron unit reversed)

item.title: Jimmy Neutron
item.content:encoded: Hey look! They found Jimmy's time machine!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I first read this a while ago, but I just realized something that nobody else seems to have commented on. The microwave's power level acts as the exponent, right? So, setting it to, say, 30 seconds with a power of two, ages it 30*30 seconds, or 30 seconds to the power of two.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well someone had to buy it. He probably bought it because he needed a new microwave. Of course, upon discovery of its anomalous properties, he did his job as an SCP agent and brought it into containment.

No doubt there's several times more anomalous objects bought by other people who don't work for SCP—perhaps people who use them recklessly because they don't know about the properties or understand the consequences, or even people who plan to use them for evil.

EDIT: I just looked at the test logs again, and the first couple experiments all but confirm this is the case, at least the first part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: QUACK QUACK. I've always said that microwave ovens are dangerous. :P QUACK QUACK.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just not a fan of agents buying things at a yard sale (or where-ever) and THEN realizing it's an anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I still absolutely adore this one, especially the test logs.

One note, though:

"Furthermore, due to the possibility of micro-organisms undergoing accelerated evolution within SCP-119, industrial-grade disinfectant has now been added to the containment procedure for SCP-119."

This seems like a REALLY easy way to get some very nasty disinfectant-resistant bacteria. Though I suppose I can't think of a better solution here… still, I figured I'd note it on the off chance that anyone has a better idea.

item.title: Image Removal
item.content:encoded: SCP-119's image was removed because we could not properly determine whether or not the image was properly licensed or attributed. It may be added once again if/when proper attribution or proper use under CC guidelines is confirmed. Please contact (Devereaux) or another member of the Image Team if you have questions.

item.title: Re: + 100
item.content:encoded: This one makes me happy, I'm happy for it. Good job elrosth!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, when I read the title my first thought was 'man, that is such a better name.'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
El Psy Congeroo.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was going to downvote until the last entry in the test log. Love that part

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just nitpicking here, but I think the value the Foundation would use would differ from that slightly. According to the SI (which I assume the Foundation will follow because of scientific unambiguity) a year has, on average, 365.24219 SI days that consist of 86400 seconds each. So the amount of years should be, IMHO,

999^5/(86400*365.24219) ≈ 31,530,638 years

which makes a difference of approximately 674 years.

Also, I do realize the post I'm replying to is almost a year old but there are only two posts below this one in the entire comment section so replying to the actual discussion around the exact value seems to be the most obvious choice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jeez, this is a first SCP? Congrats to the author, you have a well earned +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of my favorites. And it's a first SCP? Amazing. Truly amazing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! Yeah, I was a bit hasty with my math overall on this thing. Editor assistance is greatly appreciated. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I corrected a bit of math in the article (I assume this is a minor edit like correcting a typo, and thus does not require permission).

999^5 seconds would be 31,529,964 years, not 31,551,559 years.

999^5 = 995,009,990,004,999 seconds.
995009990004999/60 = 16,583,499,833,416.65 minutes
16583499833416.65/60 = 276,391,663,890.2775 hours
276391663890.2775/24 = 11,516,319,328.7615625 days
11516319328.7615625/365.25 ≈ 31,529,963.94 years

I think what happened is that you didn't account for leap day. It's still over thirty-one million years, though. On the other hand, it does change the rounded number from 31.6 million to 31.5 million.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one. Simple, but with interesting implications.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes - the timer counts down, the internal light turns on, and it makes a humming noise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just want to be clear on this: at a power level of one, does it do anything at all?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: O.o I am surprised and impressed. It's a time-microwave, but what saves it is how much you've gone into the details of what this does to stuff you put into it, especially the part where they were expecting it to cleanly age an animal and instead instant death.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Granted, I also thought it was a really bad name at first, but eh. Names are less a part of canon than everything else that is not canon. It's not called the timecrowave anywhere but the list, which has loads of silly names anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not since Mikey liked Life cereal has my worldview been so jarred. :o

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. Partly to avoid confusion with the SNL skit, partly because it's a really stupid name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not bad. Upvoted.

Also, since you are new, you should understand that this is high praise, especially for a first SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On that note though, would I have a better shot if I changed the name?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just the title is from that. I thought of this SCP before the skit, and I liked the name. Besides, the function of the SNL Timecrowave is to send food backwards in time, not age things forward.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not badly written, but this is straight out of a recent SNL skit with Alec Baldwin. Alec Baldwin for god's sake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Y'know, I came into this expecting to downvote. I mean, "timecrowave"? Seriously? But it's actually a fairly good SCP. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually quite like it. I mean, what happens if you don't clean it out?
